era_context: Early 14th century: The Mamluk Sultanate is consolidating power in the Eastern Mediterranean; the Yuan Dynasty is experiencing internal instability; the Kingdom of France under Philip IV is at the height of its power; the Byzantine Empire is fragmented and declining; the Mali Empire is consolidating control over West African trade; the Hundred Years' War has not yet begun but tensions between England and France are rising.

events.title: Battle of Courtrai (Battle of the Golden Spurs)
events.region: County of Flanders / Kingdom of France
events.description: On 11 July 1302, Flemish militia forces defeated a French cavalry army at the Battle of Courtrai (also known as the Battle of the Golden Spurs) in Flanders. The Flemish victory, achieved by infantry armed with pikes against French mounted knights, was a significant military upset and demonstrated that disciplined infantry could defeat feudal cavalry. The battle was fought during the conflict between the County of Flanders and the Kingdom of France over control of the region. The Flemish victory enhanced the independence of Flanders and contributed to the decline of feudal cavalry as the dominant military force in Europe. The battle is named 'Golden Spurs' from the golden spurs of the defeated French knights that were collected as trophies.

sources.name: Contemporary chronicles: Froissart, Jean de Joinville

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Courtrai on 11 July 1302 is well-documented in contemporary chronicles and later sources. The date and basic facts are secure.
cross_references: 1305-001 (Clement V elected, related to French power)

events.title: Estates-General of France Convened
events.description: King Philip IV of France convened the Estates-General (États-Généraux) in April 1302 to seek support for his conflict with Pope Boniface VIII over taxation of the clergy. The Estates-General was an assembly of representatives from the three estates (clergy, nobility, and commoners) and represented an early form of representative government in France. Philip's convocation of the Estates-General was a significant assertion of royal power and a demonstration of his ability to mobilize support from all segments of society against the papacy. The assembly supported Philip's position, strengthening his hand in his conflict with Boniface VIII. The Estates-General would become an important institution in French governance, though it would not develop into a true parliament as in England.
events.certainty_note: The convocation of the Estates-General in April 1302 is well-documented in French royal records and contemporary chronicles.
cross_references: 1303-002 (Anagni Incident, related to Philip IV's conflict with papacy)

events.title: Ghazan Khan Converts to Islam
events.description: Ghazan Khan, the eighth Ilkhan (ruler of the Ilkhanate), formally converted to Islam in 1302, adopting the Islamic name Mahmud. Ghazan's conversion was a significant moment in the history of the Ilkhanate, as it marked the formal integration of the Mongol ruling elite into Islamic civilization. The conversion was not purely religious but also political, as it strengthened Ghazan's legitimacy among his Persian and Islamic subjects. Ghazan's reign (1295–1304) was marked by administrative reforms, military campaigns, and cultural patronage. His conversion represented the broader process of Mongol assimilation into Islamic and Persian culture, a process that would eventually lead to the dissolution of the Ilkhanate and the emergence of Persian and Islamic successor states.
events.certainty_note: Ghazan Khan's conversion to Islam in 1302 is well-documented in Ilkhanate and Persian sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Clement V Elected Pope (Alternative Dating)
events.description: Some sources date the election of Clement V (Bertrand de Got) to 1302 rather than 1305. The exact date of his election is disputed, with sources varying between 1302 and 1305. If the 1302 date is correct, Clement V's election would have occurred during the conflict between Philip IV and Pope Boniface VIII, making his election even more significant as a victory for the French king. However, the more widely accepted date is June 1305. This entry is included to note the dating uncertainty.
events.certainty_note: The election date of Clement V is disputed between 1302 and 1305. Most modern scholarship places it in June 1305, but some sources suggest 1302. This uncertainty reflects gaps in contemporary documentation.
cross_references: 1305-001 (Clement V's election, more widely accepted date)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Courtrai is sometimes portrayed as a decisive turning point in military history, marking the end of feudal cavalry dominance. However, modern scholarship notes that cavalry remained important in European warfare for centuries after 1302, and the battle's significance has been somewhat exaggerated in popular historiography. The Estates-General of 1302 is sometimes presented as an early parliament, but it lacked the institutional continuity and legislative power of the English Parliament. The dating of Clement V's election is disputed, with some sources placing it in 1302 and others in 1305, introducing uncertainty into the historical record.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1302 is rich for Western Europe (French, Flemish, and papal sources) and moderate for the Islamic world. The Battle of Courtrai is extensively documented in contemporary chronicles and later sources. The Estates-General is well-documented in French royal records. The conversion of Ghazan Khan is documented in Ilkhanate and Persian sources. The year is significant primarily for the Battle of Courtrai and the Estates-General, both of which reflect broader shifts in European political and military organization. The historiographic bias toward Western Europe is evident in the prominence given to these events. The dating uncertainty regarding Clement V's election reflects gaps in contemporary documentation and the challenges of medieval chronology.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Courtrai and Estates-General both occurred in 1302, reflecting French political and military challenges
Estates-General support strengthened Philip IV's position against Boniface VIII, leading to Anagni Incident
Ghazan Khan's conversion and the Mamluk-Mongol conflict occurred in the same period
Disputed dating of Clement V's election (1302 vs. 1305)
